news_summary: Today's space discoveries showcase groundbreaking insights into unique planetary compositions, ancient cosmic phenomena, and interstellar objects, deepening our understanding of the universe's origins and mysteries.
news_summary_fr: Les découvertes spatiales d'aujourd'hui nous apportent des connaissances révolutionnaires sur la composition unique des planètes, les phénomènes cosmiques anciens et les objets interstellaires, approfondissant ainsi notre compréhension des origines et des mystères de l'univers.
news_summary_es: Los descubrimientos espaciales actuales nos ofrecen conocimientos revolucionarios sobre la composición única de los planetas, los fenómenos cósmicos ancestrales y los objetos interestelares, lo que nos permite profundizar en nuestra comprensión de los orígenes y los misterios del universo.

articles.title: JWST reveals a strange sulfur world unlike any planet we know
articles.summary: Astronomers have identified a strange new kind of exoplanet that challenges how scientists classify worlds beyond our Solar System. The planet, L 98-59 d, appears to contain a vast ocean of molten rock beneath its surface that traps large amounts of sulfur deep inside. Observations from the James Webb Space Telescope revealed unusual sulfur-rich gases in its atmosphere and a surprisingly low density for its size.
articles.content:
A research team led by the University of Oxford has uncovered evidence for a previously unknown kind of planet beyond our Solar System -- one that locks away large quantities of sulfur deep inside a long lasting ocean of molten rock. The results were published on March 16 in Nature Astronomy.

The world, called L 98-59 d (an exoplanet, meaning it orbits a star outside our Solar System), circles a small red star about 35 light-years from Earth. Data from the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) and ground based observatories revealed something unusual. For a planet about 1.6 times the size of Earth, it has a surprisingly low density and an atmosphere rich in hydrogen sulfide.

A Planet That Defies Classification

Until now, scientists would have grouped a planet like L 98-59 d into one of two categories. It could be a rocky "gas-dwarf" with a hydrogen dominated atmosphere, or a water rich world covered by deep oceans and ice.

New evidence shows it fits neither category. Instead, L 98-59 d appears to belong to a completely different class of planet dominated by heavy sulfur compounds.

A Global Magma Ocean Beneath the Surface

To understand this unusual world, researchers from the University of Oxford, the University of Groningen, the University of Leeds and ETH Zurich used advanced computer simulations to trace its evolution from shortly after formation to today, spanning nearly five billion years. By combining telescope observations with detailed models of planetary interiors and atmospheres, they were able to infer what is happening deep inside the planet.

Their findings suggest that L 98-59 d has a mantle made of molten silicate, similar to lava on Earth. Beneath its surface lies a vast magma ocean extending thousands of kilometers deep. This enormous reservoir allows the planet to trap large amounts of sulfur within its interior over long periods of time.

The magma ocean also helps maintain a thick hydrogen rich atmosphere that contains sulfur bearing gases such as hydrogen sulfide (H2S). Normally, radiation from the host star would gradually strip these gases away into space through X-ray driven processes.

Sulfur Cycling Between Interior and Atmosphere

Over billions of years, ongoing chemical exchanges between the molten interior and the atmosphere have shaped the planet's current appearance. These interactions explain the unusual signals detected by telescopes.

Researchers suggest that L 98-59 d may be the first identified example of a broader population of gas rich sulfur dominated planets that sustain long lived magma oceans. If that is the case, it points to a much wider variety of planetary types across the galaxy than previously recognized.

Lead author Dr. Harrison Nicholls (Department of Physics, University of Oxford) said: "This discovery suggests that the categories astronomers currently use to describe small planets may be too simple. While this molten planet is unlikely to support life, it reflects the wide diversity of the worlds which exist beyond the Solar System. We may then ask: what other types of planet are waiting to be uncovered?"

How Sulfur Shapes the Atmosphere

JWST observations from 2024 detected sulfur dioxide along with other sulfur gases high in the upper atmosphere of L 98-59 d. According to the team's models, these gases form when ultraviolet radiation from the host star, the red dwarf L 98-59, drives chemical reactions.

At the same time, the magma ocean below acts as a massive storage system for volatile materials, absorbing and releasing gases over billions of years after the planet formed. This combination of deep interior storage and ultraviolet driven chemistry explains the planet's distinctive properties.

Simulations indicate that L 98-59 d likely formed with a large supply of volatile material and may once have resembled a larger sub-Neptune type planet. Over time, it cooled, lost part of its atmosphere, and became smaller.

Scientists note that magma oceans are thought to be the initial state of all rocky planets (including the Earth and Mars). Studying these environments on distant worlds can provide insight into the earliest stages of our own planet's history.

Reconstructing Alien Worlds With Models

Co-author Professor Raymond Pierrehumbert (Department of Physics, University of Oxford) said: "What's exciting is that we can use computer models to uncover the hidden interior of a planet we will never visit. Although astronomers can only measure a planet's size, mass and atmospheric composition from afar, this research shows that it is possible to reconstruct the deep past of these alien worlds -- and discover types of planets with no equivalent in our own Solar System."

JWST is already delivering a growing stream of data, and future missions such as Ariel and PLATO are expected to expand that dataset even further. The research team plans to apply their models to these observations using machine learning to map the diversity of planets beyond our Solar System and link them to their early development.

By doing so, scientists hope to better understand how planets form and evolve, and to identify which types of worlds might be capable of supporting life.

Dr. Richard Chatterjee (University of Leeds/ University of Oxford) said: "Our computer models simulate various planetary processes, effectively enabling us to turn back the clock and understand how this unusual rocky exoplanet, L 98-59 d, evolved. Hydrogen sulfide gas, responsible for the smell of rotten eggs, appears to play a starring role there. But, as always, more observations are needed to understand this planet and others like it. Further investigation may yet show that rather pungent planets are surprisingly common."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a new class of exoplanet with a sulfur-rich magma ocean, expanding our understanding of planetary diversity beyond the Solar System. This breakthrough has broad implications for planetary science and the search for life, supported by detailed observations and modeling, thus meeting the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: James Webb telescope finds a new type of sulfur-rich planet with a molten rock ocean
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study planets outside our Solar System, called exoplanets, to learn how they form and change. Usually, they group these planets as rocky worlds or water-rich planets.

**What happened** – Using the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), astronomers discovered an unusual planet named L 98-59 d, about 35 light-years from Earth. This planet is 1.6 times bigger than Earth but has a much lower density. It has a thick atmosphere rich in sulfur gases like hydrogen sulfide and a huge ocean of molten rock beneath its surface.

**Impact** – L 98-59 d doesn’t fit into known planet types. Instead, it belongs to a new class of planets with deep magma oceans trapping sulfur inside. This discovery shows that planets can be more diverse than scientists thought. It helps us understand how planets form and evolve, including the early history of rocky planets like Earth.

**What's next step** – Researchers will use models and more telescope data to study other planets, searching for more sulfur-rich worlds. Future missions will gather more information to help map out the variety of planets in our galaxy and learn which might support life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The James Webb Space Telescope has revealed a new kind of planet with a sulfur-rich molten rock ocean, expanding our knowledge of the many different worlds beyond our Solar System.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le télescope James Webb découvre une nouvelle planète riche en soufre avec un océan de roche en fusion
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient les exoplanètes, des planètes situées en dehors de notre Système solaire, pour comprendre leur formation et leur évolution. Habituellement, ils classent ces planètes en mondes rocheux ou riches en eau.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce au télescope spatial James Webb (JWST), des astronomes ont découvert une planète inhabituelle appelée L 98-59 d, située à environ 35 années-lumière de la Terre. Cette planète, 1,6 fois plus grande que la Terre, a une densité beaucoup plus faible. Elle possède une atmosphère épaisse riche en gaz soufrés comme le sulfure d’hydrogène, et un immense océan de roche en fusion sous sa surface.

**Impact** – L 98-59 d ne correspond à aucune catégorie connue de planètes. Elle appartient à une nouvelle classe de planètes avec un océan de magma profond qui emprisonne le soufre à l’intérieur. Cette découverte montre que les planètes sont plus diverses que ce que les scientifiques pensaient. Cela aide à mieux comprendre la formation et l’évolution des planètes, y compris l’histoire ancienne des planètes rocheuses comme la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront des modèles informatiques et plus de données des télescopes pour étudier d’autres planètes et trouver d’autres mondes riches en soufre. Les futures missions permettront de collecter davantage d’informations pour mieux connaître la diversité des planètes dans notre galaxie et identifier celles qui pourraient abriter la vie.

**Une phrase clé** – Le télescope James Webb a révélé un nouveau type de planète avec un océan de roche en fusion riche en soufre, élargissant notre connaissance des nombreux mondes au-delà de notre Système solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: El telescopio James Webb descubre un nuevo planeta rico en azufre con un océano de roca fundida
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian exoplanetas, que son planetas fuera de nuestro Sistema Solar, para entender cómo se forman y cambian. Normalmente, los clasifican en planetas rocosos o ricos en agua.

**Qué pasó** – Usando el telescopio espacial James Webb (JWST), los astrónomos encontraron un planeta extraño llamado L 98-59 d, a unos 35 años luz de la Tierra. Este planeta es 1.6 veces más grande que la Tierra pero tiene una densidad mucho menor. Tiene una atmósfera con muchos gases de azufre como el sulfuro de hidrógeno y un enorme océano de roca fundida bajo su superficie.

**Impacto** – L 98-59 d no encaja en los tipos de planetas conocidos. Pertenece a una nueva clase de planetas con océanos de magma que atrapan azufre en su interior. Este descubrimiento muestra que los planetas pueden ser más variados de lo que se pensaba. Ayuda a entender cómo se forman y evolucionan los planetas, incluso la historia temprana de planetas rocosos como la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán modelos y más datos de telescopios para estudiar otros planetas y buscar más mundos ricos en azufre. Las futuras misiones recogerán más información para mapear la diversidad de planetas en nuestra galaxia y descubrir cuáles podrían tener vida.

**Una frase clave** – El telescopio James Webb ha descubierto un nuevo tipo de planeta con un océano de roca fundida rica en azufre, ampliando nuestro conocimiento sobre los diferentes mundos fuera de nuestro Sistema Solar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a glowing, molten exoplanet with a vast, fiery magma ocean beneath its cracked surface, surrounded by a swirling, translucent atmosphere infused with wisps of yellow-green sulfur gases, orbiting a warm red dwarf star in the background, rendered with warm, earthy tones and soft, natural colors.

articles.title: Rare supernova from 10 billion years ago may reveal the secret of dark energy
articles.summary: Astronomers may have found an exciting new clue about dark energy—the mysterious force driving the universe’s accelerating expansion. They discovered an extraordinarily bright supernova from more than 10 billion years ago whose light was bent and magnified by a foreground galaxy, creating multiple images through gravitational lensing. Because the light from each image traveled slightly different paths, it arrived at Earth at different times, letting scientists effectively watch different moments of the same cosmic explosion simultaneously.
articles.content:
Astronomers may be getting closer to understanding one of the biggest mysteries in cosmology: dark energy, the unknown force believed to be driving the accelerating expansion of the universe.

Dark energy is thought to account for about 68% of the universe. Despite its enormous influence, scientists still do not know what it is or exactly how it shapes the growth of the cosmos.

Now researchers have identified an unusual supernova in the early Universe that could provide a valuable new clue. The light from this powerful explosion has been traveling toward Earth for more than 10 billion years. The event was exceptionally bright, and its light was further amplified by the gravity of a galaxy along the line of sight, making the distant explosion appear even more luminous.

"No one has found a supernova like this before, and the nature of the system means it may be able to help solve some big problems in astrophysics such as the nature of the force that drives the expansion of the universe," explains Dr. Daniel Perley, a reader in astrophysics at Liverpool John Moores University.

Gravity Splits the Light Into Multiple Images

A galaxy positioned directly between Earth and the distant supernova plays a crucial role in the discovery. Its gravitational pull bends the light traveling from the explosion toward us.

"We are seeing the light from this distant supernova split into multiple images, what we call 'gravitationally lensed'," explains Jacob Wise, a PhD student at the Astrophysics Research Institute, who first recognized the importance of the event.

Light's varying paths to Earth

"When light is 'lensed', the different paths the light follows to get to Earth don't all have the same length, so light moving along different paths takes variable amounts of time to reach us."

Because a supernova can shine for several months, astronomers can observe multiple images of the same explosion at once. Each image represents the supernova at a slightly different moment in its evolution because the light traveled along paths of different lengths.

"What's exciting about that is that the amount of time difference between different images depends on the expansion rate of the universe," added Dr. Perley.

The research team, working with collaborators at Caltech, Stockholm University, and other institutions around the world, plans to measure these time delays with high precision. Those measurements could reveal how quickly the universe is expanding and provide insight into the force (dark energy) responsible for accelerating that expansion.

A Potential Tie Breaker in the Hubble Tension

Astronomers currently face a major puzzle about the universe's expansion rate. Different methods produce conflicting values for the Hubble constant, the number used to describe how fast the universe is expanding.

Perley believes observations of this unusual supernova could help resolve the disagreement.

He said: "Studies of afterglow of the Big Bang give one number for the so-called Hubble constant -- the measurement of the expansion speed of the universe -- while studies of nearby galaxies give a different number. Astronomers are calling this the Hubble Tension. Hence, studies of lensed supernovae could indicate which of these two numbers we should really believe."

Observatories Around the World Join the Study

The supernova's brightness allowed astronomers to detect it even from its enormous distance using medium sized ground based telescopes. These included the Zwicky Transient Facility in California and the Liverpool Telescope in La Palma.

(The first telescope to detect the supernova was the Zwicky Transient Facility in California, but it couldn't see the multiple images. The Liverpool Telescope was the first facility to see the multiple images -- and thereby prove it was gravitationally lensed)

The object was later studied in greater detail using some of the world's most powerful observatories, including the Keck Telescopes in Hawaii, the Hubble Space Telescope, and the James Webb Space Telescope.

Added Jacob: "Our colleagues in Stockholm first noticed the supernova but it was us who spotted that the light had been bent into multiple images.

"All the major observatories in the Northern Hemisphere plus the space-based telescopes have been looking at this but it was the Liverpool Telescope, run from LJMU, that got there first," beamed Wise.

The study "Discovery of SN 2025wny: A Strongly Gravitationally Lensed Superluminous Supernova at z = 2.01" was published in the journal Astrophysical Letters and authored by Joel Johansson, Daniel A. Perley, Ariel Goobar, Jacob L. Wise, Yu-Jing Qin, Zoë McGrath, Steve Schulze, Cameron Lemon, Anjasha Gangopadhyay, Konstantinos Tsalapatas, and 24 other co-authors.
articles.reasoning: The discovery of a rare, gravitationally lensed supernova from 10 billion years ago offers a significant new method to measure the universe's expansion rate and understand dark energy, a major unresolved question in cosmology. This breakthrough has broad implications for astrophysics and could resolve the current Hubble tension, benefiting the scientific community and humanity's understanding of the cosmos.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers discover rare supernova that may unlock secrets of dark energy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long been puzzled by dark energy, a mysterious force that makes the universe expand faster and faster. Despite making up most of the universe, dark energy remains one of the biggest mysteries in space science.

**What happened** – A team of astronomers found a very bright supernova, an exploding star, from over 10 billion years ago. This supernova’s light was bent and magnified by a galaxy between it and Earth, creating several images of the same explosion. Because the light traveled different paths, each image arrived at a different time, letting scientists watch the supernova’s changes from different moments.

**Impact** – This rare event helps scientists measure how fast the universe is expanding more accurately. It could also solve a big disagreement among astronomers about the universe’s expansion speed, known as the Hubble tension. By studying the time delays between the images, researchers hope to learn more about dark energy and how it controls the universe’s growth.

**What's next step** – The team will use powerful telescopes worldwide to carefully measure the timing of the supernova’s multiple images. These measurements will improve our understanding of the universe’s expansion and might help decide which current estimates of the expansion rate are correct.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A unique supernova from 10 billion years ago, seen through a natural cosmic lens, offers new clues to understanding dark energy and the universe’s expanding speed.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronomes découvrent une supernova rare qui pourrait révéler les secrets de l'énergie noire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques sont depuis longtemps intrigués par l'énergie noire, une force mystérieuse qui accélère l'expansion de l'univers. Bien qu'elle constitue la majeure partie de l'univers, l'énergie noire reste un grand mystère en astronomie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe d'astronomes a découvert une supernova très brillante, une étoile en explosion, datant de plus de 10 milliards d'années. La lumière de cette supernova a été déviée et amplifiée par une galaxie située entre elle et la Terre, créant plusieurs images de la même explosion. Comme la lumière a suivi différents chemins, chaque image est arrivée à des moments différents, permettant aux scientifiques d'observer la supernova à différents stades.

**Impact** – Cet événement rare aide les scientifiques à mesurer plus précisément la vitesse d'expansion de l'univers. Il pourrait aussi résoudre un important désaccord entre astronomes sur cette vitesse, appelé la tension de Hubble. En étudiant les décalages temporels entre les images, les chercheurs espèrent mieux comprendre l'énergie noire et son rôle dans la croissance de l'univers.

**Prochaine étape** – L'équipe utilisera des télescopes puissants dans le monde entier pour mesurer précisément le temps d'arrivée des différentes images de la supernova. Ces mesures amélioreront notre compréhension de l'expansion de l'univers et pourraient déterminer quelle estimation actuelle est la bonne.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Une supernova unique vieille de 10 milliards d'années, observée grâce à une lentille cosmique naturelle, offre de nouveaux indices pour comprendre l'énergie noire et la vitesse d'expansion de l'univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos descubren una supernova rara que podría revelar los secretos de la energía oscura
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos llevan mucho tiempo intrigados por la energía oscura, una fuerza misteriosa que hace que el universo se expanda cada vez más rápido. Aunque representa la mayor parte del universo, la energía oscura sigue siendo un gran misterio en la astronomía.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de astrónomos encontró una supernova muy brillante, una estrella explotando, de hace más de 10 mil millones de años. La luz de esta supernova fue doblada y aumentada por una galaxia situada entre ella y la Tierra, creando varias imágenes de la misma explosión. Como la luz viajó por diferentes caminos, cada imagen llegó en distintos momentos, permitiendo a los científicos observar la supernova en diferentes etapas.

**Impacto** – Este evento raro ayuda a los científicos a medir con más precisión la velocidad a la que se expande el universo. También podría resolver una gran diferencia entre astrónomos sobre esta velocidad, llamada la tensión de Hubble. Al estudiar los retrasos en el tiempo entre las imágenes, los investigadores esperan entender mejor la energía oscura y cómo controla el crecimiento del universo.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo usará telescopios poderosos en todo el mundo para medir con cuidado el tiempo de llegada de las múltiples imágenes de la supernova. Estas medidas mejorarán nuestro conocimiento sobre la expansión del universo y podrían ayudar a decidir cuál de las estimaciones actuales es correcta.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una supernova única de hace 10 mil millones de años, vista a través de una lente cósmica natural, ofrece nuevas pistas para entender la energía oscura y la velocidad de expansión del universo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a luminous supernova explosion in deep space, its radiant light bending and splitting into multiple glowing arcs around a silhouetted galaxy acting as a gravitational lens, set against a softly colored cosmic background symbolizing the expanding universe.

articles.title: This massive crater could expose the heart of a lost planet
articles.summary: A mysterious metal-rich asteroid called Psyche has been baffling scientists for over two centuries, and its true origin remains one of the biggest unanswered questions in planetary science. Is it the exposed core of a failed planet, or a chaotic mix of rock and metal forged through countless violent collisions? To find out, researchers simulated how a massive crater near Psyche’s north pole formed, revealing that the asteroid’s internal “porosity” — how much empty space it contains — may hold the key to its secrets.
articles.content:
More than two centuries after asteroid 16 Psyche was first identified, scientists are still trying to determine how it formed.

Located in the main asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter, Psyche ranks as the 10th-most massive asteroid and the largest known object made primarily of metal, measuring about 140 miles across. NASA's Psyche spacecraft is scheduled to arrive in 2029 with the goal of uncovering its origin. Researchers suspect it could be a remnant of an early planet that was torn apart by massive collisions, or possibly a fragment of a once-layered body that lost its outer rocky shell.

Other ideas suggest Psyche may have formed as a metal-rich object from the start, or that it became a mix of rock and metal after repeated impacts with other asteroids. Each possibility points to a different story about how planets formed in the early Solar System.

Simulating Craters to Reveal Psyche's Interior

To explore these possibilities, scientists at the University of Arizona's Lunar and Planetary Laboratory created simulations to understand how a large crater near Psyche's north pole may have formed. Their findings, published in JGR Planets, provide predictions that will help researchers interpret data collected by NASA's Psyche mission when it arrives. By combining these simulations with real observations, scientists hope to finally determine what Psyche is made of.

"Large impact basins or craters excavate deep into the asteroid, which gives clues about what its interior is made of," said Namya Baijal, a doctoral candidate at the LPL and first author of the paper. "By simulating the formation of one of its largest craters, we were able to make testable predictions for Psyche's overall composition when the spacecraft arrives."

Although metal-rich asteroids make up less than 10% of the main belt, Psyche is the largest among them. Still, researchers will need direct spacecraft measurements to understand how that metal is distributed throughout its interior.

Why Porosity Matters in Asteroid Impacts

"One of our main findings was that the porosity -- the amount of empty space inside the asteroid -- plays a significant role in how these craters form," said Baijal. "Porosity is often ignored because it's difficult to include in models, but our simulations show it can strongly affect the impact process and shape of craters left behind."

Asteroids with more internal voids tend to absorb impact energy more effectively, which leads to deeper and steeper craters and less debris scattered across the surface. By comparing simulated crater features with those observed by the spacecraft, scientists can test whether Psyche's interior is layered, with distinct metal and rock regions, or instead a more chaotic mixture of materials.

Clues to Planet Formation in the Early Solar System

The research team compares their approach to examining the remains of an abandoned pizza shop. Psyche and other main belt asteroids are thought to be leftover building blocks from planet formation. "The cooks have long left, but you can look at what's left behind -- the ovens, scraps of dough, the toppings -- and make inferences about how the pizzas were made," said Erik Asphaug, a professor in LPL and co-author of the study. "We can't get to the cores of Earth or Mars or Venus, but maybe we can get to the core of an early asteroid."

If Psyche turns out to be the exposed core of a former planet, stripped of its outer layers, it would provide rare insight into a violent phase of planetary evolution that is otherwise impossible to observe directly.

"We tested two main interior structures for Psyche," said Baijal. "One is a layered structure with a metallic core and a thin, rocky mantle, which likely formed if a violent collision stripped away the outer layers. The other is a uniform mixture of metal and silicate, created by a more catastrophic impact that mixed everything together, like some metal-rich meteorites found on Earth."

Modeling a Massive Impact on Psyche

Using detailed shape models based on telescope data, the researchers built a 3D representation of Psyche and recreated the formation of a large crater roughly 30 miles wide and three miles deep. In their simulations, the asteroid was struck at speeds typical for the asteroid belt, about three miles per second. They tested different impactor sizes and compared two internal structure models (metallic core and mixed rock-and-metal) to see which could best match the observed crater.

"We found that an impactor about three miles across would create a crater of the right dimensions," Baijal said. "The crater's formation is consistent with both scenarios of Psyche's makeup."

Unlike planets, many asteroids are not solid. Instead, they often contain fractured material and empty spaces from past collisions. By including porosity in their simulations, the researchers showed that it has a major influence on crater formation and how debris is distributed after an impact.

"By rigorously treating Psyche's shape, porosity and composition, this work represents a true watershed moment for our capacity to realistically simulate impacts into unique types of asteroids," said Adeene Denton, a postdoctoral researcher and another co-author of the study.

NASA's Psyche Mission and What Comes Next

The Psyche spacecraft is equipped to measure the asteroid's surface, gravity, magnetic field, and composition. In addition to crater shapes, the simulations predict other features scientists can look for, such as density variations caused by impacts compressing the interior and the spread of metal-rich debris across the surface.

"When the spacecraft arrives at Psyche in a few years, the geochemists, geologists and modelers on the team will all be looking at the same object and trying to interpret what we see," said Asphaug. "This work gives us a head start."

The mission is led by Arizona State University, with Lindy Elkins-Tanton of the University of California, Berkeley, serving as principal investigator. NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, a division of Caltech in Pasadena, manages mission operations, system engineering, and testing. The spacecraft platform was built by Maxar Technologies (now Intuitive Machines) in Palo Alto, California.

Psyche is the 14th mission selected under NASA's Discovery Program, managed by the agency's Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama. NASA's Launch Services Program at Kennedy handled the launch.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific effort to understand the origin and composition of asteroid Psyche, which could reveal new insights into planetary formation and the early Solar System. This NASA mission and the related research have broad scientific significance and potential to advance knowledge about planetary evolution, benefiting humanity's understanding of space.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Psyche mission simulates crater to unlock asteroid’s secrets
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Psyche is a large metal-rich asteroid located between Mars and Jupiter. Scientists have been puzzled for over 200 years about whether Psyche is the exposed core of a lost planet or a mix of rock and metal formed by many collisions. NASA plans to send a spacecraft to Psyche in 2029 to study it up close.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Arizona used computer simulations to recreate how a huge crater near Psyche’s north pole was formed. They tested different ideas about Psyche’s inside, including whether it has layers or is a mixed material. Their study showed that the amount of empty space inside Psyche, called porosity, greatly affects how craters form.

**Impact** – This work helps scientists predict what the Psyche spacecraft might find when it arrives. Understanding Psyche’s interior could reveal if it is the leftover core of a planet that lost its outer layers or a jumbled mix from many impacts. This is important because Psyche may provide clues about how planets formed long ago, something we cannot study directly on Earth or other big planets.

**What’s next step** – When NASA’s Psyche spacecraft reaches the asteroid in 2029, it will measure its surface, gravity, magnetic field, and composition. Scientists will compare the spacecraft’s data with these new simulations to better understand Psyche’s makeup and history.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Simulating a giant crater on Psyche helps prepare scientists for NASA’s mission to uncover whether this metal asteroid is the core of a lost planet or a mix of rock and metal formed by collisions.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Psyche de la NASA simule un cratère pour percer les secrets de l’astéroïde
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Psyche est un grand astéroïde riche en métal situé entre Mars et Jupiter. Depuis plus de 200 ans, les scientifiques se demandent si Psyche est le noyau exposé d’une planète disparue ou un mélange de roche et de métal formé par de nombreuses collisions. La NASA prévoit d’envoyer une sonde vers Psyche en 2029 pour l’étudier de près.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université d’Arizona ont utilisé des simulations informatiques pour recréer la formation d’un énorme cratère près du pôle nord de Psyche. Ils ont testé différentes hypothèses sur l’intérieur de Psyche, notamment s’il est en couches ou un mélange uniforme. Leur étude a montré que la quantité d’espace vide à l’intérieur, appelée porosité, influence fortement la formation des cratères.

**Impact** – Ce travail aide les scientifiques à prédire ce que la sonde Psyche pourrait découvrir à son arrivée. Comprendre l’intérieur de Psyche pourrait révéler s’il s’agit du noyau laissé par une planète ayant perdu ses couches extérieures ou d’un mélange formé par de nombreux impacts. Cela est important car Psyche pourrait donner des indices sur la formation des planètes il y a longtemps, ce que nous ne pouvons pas étudier directement sur Terre ou d’autres grandes planètes.

**Prochaine étape** – Lorsque la sonde Psyche arrivera en 2029, elle mesurera la surface, la gravité, le champ magnétique et la composition de l’astéroïde. Les scientifiques compareront ces données avec les simulations pour mieux comprendre la nature et l’histoire de Psyche.

**Une phrase clé** – Simuler un grand cratère sur Psyche aide les scientifiques à se préparer à la mission de la NASA pour découvrir si cet astéroïde métallique est le noyau d’une planète disparue ou un mélange de roche et de métal formé par des collisions.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Psyche de la NASA simula un cráter para revelar los secretos del asteroide
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Psyche es un gran asteroide rico en metal ubicado entre Marte y Júpiter. Durante más de 200 años, los científicos se han preguntado si Psyche es el núcleo expuesto de un planeta perdido o una mezcla de roca y metal formada por muchas colisiones. La NASA planea enviar una nave espacial a Psyche en 2029 para estudiarlo de cerca.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Arizona usaron simulaciones por computadora para recrear cómo se formó un enorme cráter cerca del polo norte de Psyche. Probaron diferentes ideas sobre el interior de Psyche, incluyendo si tiene capas o es un material mezclado. Su estudio mostró que la cantidad de espacio vacío dentro, llamada porosidad, afecta mucho cómo se forman los cráteres.

**Impacto** – Este trabajo ayuda a los científicos a predecir qué podría encontrar la nave Psyche cuando llegue. Entender el interior de Psyche podría revelar si es el núcleo dejado por un planeta que perdió sus capas exteriores o una mezcla causada por muchos impactos. Esto es importante porque Psyche puede ofrecer pistas sobre cómo se formaron los planetas hace mucho tiempo, algo que no podemos estudiar directamente en la Tierra u otros planetas grandes.

**Próximo paso** – Cuando la nave Psyche llegue en 2029, medirá la superficie, gravedad, campo magnético y composición del asteroide. Los científicos compararán los datos con estas simulaciones para entender mejor la composición y la historia de Psyche.

**Frase clave** – Simular un cráter gigante en Psyche ayuda a preparar a los científicos para la misión de la NASA que busca descubrir si este asteroide metálico es el núcleo de un planeta perdido o una mezcla de roca y metal formada por colisiones.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a massive, metallic asteroid with a giant, deep crater near its north pole, surrounded by a sparse field of smaller rocky asteroids, illuminated softly to reveal contrasting textures of smooth metal and rough rock, symbolizing the hidden core of a lost planet and the violent impacts that shaped it.

articles.title: 3I/ATLAS: Interstellar comet has water unlike any in our solar system
articles.summary: The levels of a heavy form of hydrogen in 3I/ATLAS are 30 to 40 times higher than in Earth's oceans, suggesting the comet has a cold and distant origin
articles.content:
The levels of a heavy form of hydrogen in 3I/ATLAS are 30 to 40 times higher than in Earth's oceans, suggesting the comet has a cold and distant origin

3I/ATLAS is pretty strange International Gemini Observatory/NOIRLab/NSF/AURA/B. Bolin

The interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS contains water and carbon molecules at levels never before seen in our solar system. This suggests that it formed around an alien star radically different from and much older than the sun.

Astronomers have been tracking 3I/ATLAS since it entered our solar system last year – and it is weird. It appears to be packed with far more carbon dioxide and water than almost any other comet we have seen, and early estimates put its age at 8 billion years – almost twice as old as the sun.

Now, Martin Cordiner at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Maryland and his colleagues have found that its levels of deuterium – a form of hydrogen with an extra neutron – are at least 10 times higher than in any comet we have seen before.

Mysteries of the universe: Cheshire, England Spend a weekend with some of the brightest minds in science, as you explore the mysteries of the universe in an exciting programme that includes an excursion to see the iconic Lovell Telescope. Find out more

Deuterium naturally exists in small amounts in Earth’s oceans, but the levels in 3I/ATLAS are more than 40 times higher. “3I/ATLAS continues to astonish us with what it reveals about the similarities and differences of its host system compared with our own solar system,” says Cordiner. He and the team used the James Webb Space Telescope to make the observations.

“It’s really exceptional,” says Paul Hartogh at the Max Planck Institute for Solar System Research in Germany. “This deuterium-to-hydrogen ratio in water is extremely unusual, and nobody would have expected this.”

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Such high levels of deuterium are typically seen in only the coldest regions of the Milky Way, says Ewine van Dishoeck at Leiden Observatory in the Netherlands. “That means it’s probably in the very outer part of the disc around whatever star it was circling, and that makes it also easier to kick it out,” says Dishoeck.

Cordiner and his colleagues also found relatively low levels of carbon-13 – a form of carbon with an extra neutron that is typically produced after stars have exploded in a supernova. Low levels of carbon-13, which have also been found in young star-forming clouds, point to 3I/ATLAS forming at a time in the galaxy’s history when there weren’t as many polluting supernovae. This suggests the comet must have been formed around a star system around 10 billion to 12 billion years old, more than twice as old as the sun, says Cordiner.

However, Dishoeck says that the precision we have for the carbon levels means we can’t be certain about its age.

Journal reference arXiv DOI: 2603.07026
articles.reasoning: The discovery of unique water composition in the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS provides significant new insights into the formation and diversity of star systems beyond our own, expanding our understanding of the universe's history and chemistry. This is a meaningful scientific breakthrough with broad implications for astronomy and space science.
articles.personality_title: Interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS holds water unlike anything in our solar system
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been studying 3I/ATLAS, a comet that came from outside our solar system. It entered our solar system last year, and its unusual makeup has caught the attention of astronomers.

**What happened** – Using the James Webb Space Telescope, researchers found that 3I/ATLAS contains water with a very high amount of deuterium, a special form of hydrogen with an extra neutron. The levels of deuterium in this comet are 30 to 40 times higher than those found in Earth's oceans. The comet also has more carbon dioxide and water than most comets in our solar system. Scientists believe it formed around a star much older than the sun, possibly 8 to 12 billion years old.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that 3I/ATLAS comes from a very cold and distant place in space, different from our own solar system. The unusual water and carbon makeup tell us that star systems can form in many different ways, and this comet helps us learn about the history and variety of the universe. It also suggests that comets can travel long distances between stars.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue to study 3I/ATLAS and other interstellar objects to better understand their origins and the conditions in distant star systems. Future observations may reveal more about how common such comets are and what they can teach us about the galaxy’s past.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The comet 3I/ATLAS contains water with a unique chemical signature, offering new clues about the history and diversity of star systems beyond our own.
articles.personality_title_fr: La comète interstellaire 3I/ATLAS contient une eau unique dans notre système solaire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient 3I/ATLAS, une comète venue de l’extérieur de notre système solaire. Elle est entrée dans notre système l’année dernière, et sa composition étrange a attiré l’attention des astronomes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce au télescope spatial James Webb, les chercheurs ont découvert que 3I/ATLAS contient de l’eau avec une très grande quantité de deutérium, une forme spéciale d’hydrogène avec un neutron supplémentaire. Le deutérium dans cette comète est 30 à 40 fois plus élevé que dans les océans terrestres. La comète contient aussi plus de dioxyde de carbone et d’eau que la plupart des comètes du système solaire. Les scientifiques pensent qu’elle s’est formée autour d’une étoile beaucoup plus vieille que le Soleil, peut-être âgée de 8 à 12 milliards d’années.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que 3I/ATLAS vient d’un endroit très froid et lointain dans l’espace, différent de notre système solaire. La composition inhabituelle de son eau et de son carbone nous apprend que les systèmes stellaires peuvent se former de différentes façons. Cette comète nous aide à comprendre l’histoire et la diversité de l’univers, et suggère que les comètes peuvent voyager sur de grandes distances entre les étoiles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier 3I/ATLAS et d’autres objets interstellaires pour mieux comprendre leur origine et les conditions dans les systèmes stellaires éloignés. De futures observations pourraient révéler la fréquence de ces comètes et ce qu’elles nous enseignent sur le passé de la galaxie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La comète 3I/ATLAS contient une eau avec une signature chimique unique, offrant de nouveaux indices sur l’histoire et la diversité des systèmes stellaires au-delà du nôtre.
articles.personality_title_es: La cometa interestelar 3I/ATLAS tiene agua única en nuestro sistema solar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estado estudiando 3I/ATLAS, una cometa que llegó desde fuera de nuestro sistema solar. Entró en nuestro sistema el año pasado y su composición inusual llamó la atención de los astrónomos.

**Qué pasó** – Usando el telescopio espacial James Webb, los investigadores descubrieron que 3I/ATLAS contiene agua con una cantidad muy alta de deuterio, una forma especial de hidrógeno con un neutrón extra. Los niveles de deuterio en esta cometa son 30 a 40 veces mayores que en los océanos de la Tierra. La cometa también tiene más dióxido de carbono y agua que la mayoría de las cometas en nuestro sistema solar. Los científicos creen que se formó alrededor de una estrella mucho más vieja que el sol, posiblemente de 8 a 12 mil millones de años.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que 3I/ATLAS proviene de un lugar muy frío y distante en el espacio, diferente a nuestro sistema solar. La composición inusual de su agua y carbono nos enseña que los sistemas estelares pueden formarse de muchas maneras distintas. Esta cometa nos ayuda a entender la historia y la variedad del universo, y sugiere que las cometas pueden viajar grandes distancias entre estrellas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando 3I/ATLAS y otros objetos interestelares para entender mejor su origen y las condiciones en sistemas estelares distantes. Observaciones futuras podrían revelar cuán comunes son estas cometas y qué nos pueden enseñar sobre el pasado de la galaxia.

**Resumen en una frase** – La cometa 3I/ATLAS contiene agua con una firma química única, ofreciendo nuevas pistas sobre la historia y diversidad de sistemas estelares más allá del nuestro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing comet streaking gently through a starry cosmic landscape, its icy nucleus shimmering with swirling patterns of water molecules enriched with heavy hydrogen, surrounded by faint ethereal clouds representing ancient, cold regions of space, with subtle hints of distant, aged stars in the background symbolizing its origin from a far older star system.
